title: The extractor doesn't have to know anything
summary: A reported push to ban Chinese open-weight models lands the same day a 2022 Altman email surfaces in discovery arguing OpenAI should release an open model precisely to discourage competitors — openness as instrument, not position. Then Cursor's agent swarm rebuilds SQLite in Rust from the manual alone and shows the bottleneck was merge contention, not model quality; and a GraphRAG paper reports that language skill scales about fourfold with model size while factual knowledge scales twenty-one-fold, so a 7B extractor beats a 32B one.

topics: Reported US move toward restricting Chinese open-weight models (Axios via TechCrunch; Politico reports Commerce not acting soon); OpenAI's Dean W. Ball argued for a 'pretext to create regulatory fear, uncertainty, and distrust' then retracted; Sam Bresnick argues H200 export controls would bite harder

Sam Altman's October 2022 board email, surfaced in Musk v. Altman discovery: releasing a GPT-3-class local model 'helps discourage others from releasing similarly-powerful models, and makes it harder for new efforts to get funded'
Ben Thompson's counter-proposal (via Simon Willison): legislate training-data collection as fair use and bar ToS bans on distillation among domestic companies

Cursor agent swarm: planner/worker tree, custom VCS at ~1,000 commits/sec vs git's ~1,000/hour, neutral third-party conflict resolver, agent-curated Field Guide; SQLite rebuilt in Rust from documentation alone; conflicts 70,000+ -> under 1,000; 9,908 lines vs 64,305; Opus 4.8 planner + Composer 2.5 worker $1,339 vs GPT-5.5 both roles $10,565

RAGU (Novosibirsk State University / ITMO): GraphRAG engine separating extraction from consolidation; language skills scale ~4x vs factual knowledge ~21x across 0.5B-72B; Meno-Lite-0.1 (7B) beats Qwen2.5-32B on graph construction by 12.5% relative harmonic mean; GraphRAG-Bench Medical evidence recall up to 0.84 vs <=0.76; HippoRAG2 multi-hop lead shown to be an answer-format artifact; ~$0.001/doc vs ~$0.10/doc

threads.name: AI sovereignty and open weights
threads.status: The Trump administration is reportedly weighing restrictions on Chinese open-weight models including Kimi K3, with American frontier labs pushing for it; Politico reports Commerce won't act imminently, and no mechanism has been specified. Ben Thompson counter-proposes legislating training-data fair use and banning ToS prohibitions on domestic distillation.
threads.arc: From a cost-and-sovereignty case for open weights, to a security-refusal case, to an outright policy fight over whose open weights are allowed — with a 2022 Altman email revealing that openness was a competitive instrument at OpenAI from the start, not a principle that was later abandoned.

repeat_coverage.story: Kimi K3 and the open-weight policy fight
repeat_coverage.repeat_of: 2026-07-17 'Kimi K3: a 2.8-trillion-parameter model whose weights arrive in ten days' / active thread 'AI sovereignty and open weights'
repeat_coverage.new_development: A specific reported US policy move — Axios reporting the administration is considering banning Kimi K3 and other Chinese models, an OpenAI staffer's since-retracted call for a regulatory 'pretext', and Politico's contrary report — plus Altman's October 2022 board email newly surfaced through Musk v. Altman discovery.

dives.story: The reported open-weight ban push, and Altman's 2022 email
dives.why_it_matters: Anyone whose stack currently depends on Chinese open weights — a large share of cost-sensitive US teams — needs to know a restriction is being lobbied for, and the 2022 email tells them openness at the frontier labs has always been an instrument rather than a commitment, which predicts how the argument recurs.

dives.story: Cursor's agent swarm: the bottleneck was merge contention, not model quality
dives.why_it_matters: Anyone running more than a handful of coding agents on one repo: the failure mode is write contention (7,771 conflicts in one file touched by 1,173 agents), and the cost lever is the planner/worker model split — 7.9x cheaper for the same passing result.

dives.story: RAGU: why a 7B extractor beats a 32B one
dives.why_it_matters: Anyone building retrieval or document-ingestion pipelines: if language skill really scales ~4x while world knowledge scales ~21x, the frontier model doing your extraction step is buying knowledge the task doesn't use — at roughly a hundred times the per-document cost.

concepts_taught: why language skill and factual world knowledge scale differently with model size (~4x vs ~21x across 0.5B-72B), and why that makes a small model the right choice for in-pipeline extraction
write contention as the real bottleneck in multi-agent coding, and the planner/worker role split as both a context-economy and a cost-allocation mechanism

lore.note: Alan's twenty-dollar weekend-agent allowance is now his unit of account for agent spending — he priced Cursor's $1,339 swarm run at 'sixty-seven weekends' and declined.
